summary: In this podcast episode, Dr. Terrie E. Moffitt discusses her longitudinal research on executive function, self-control, and mental health over the lifespan. She explains how early childhood adversity and cognitive processing styles predict vaccine hesitancy and resistance. Furthermore, she reveals that mental health disorders are nearly universal over a lifetime and highlights the unreliability of human retrospective recall.

principles.statement: Self-control is highly malleable over a person's lifetime.
principles.rationale: Unlike IQ, which remains relatively stable from childhood to adulthood, longitudinal data shows that individuals' self-control scores change significantly in response to their decisions, goals, and plans.
principles.source_quote: their self-control is something that responds to their decisions and their their um uh as you say go making goals and plans so that's that malleability is very interesting

principles.statement: Laboratory tests fail to accurately measure real-world executive function.
principles.rationale: People often score well in quiet, structured clinical settings but fail to use those same executive functions in the real world when they are tired, multitasking, or under stress.
principles.source_quote: the same person who scored well on our psychological test doesn't do so well when they're tired when they're multitasking... when under stress that's when executive functions rubber hits the road

principles.statement: Mental health disorders are the norm, not the exception, over a lifespan.
principles.rationale: By conducting full psychiatric assessments regularly over decades, research shows that 85% of people experience at least one mental disorder by the time they reach their 50s.
principles.source_quote: if you haven't had your turn at mental disorder yet it's probably coming to you

principles.statement: Human retrospective recall is fundamentally flawed.
principles.rationale: When adults are asked to remember their childhoods, there is extremely low agreement with their actual historical data; they often forget past mental health struggles and inflate their past abilities.
principles.source_quote: people simply do not remember their childhood and Adolescent adolescence the way it was

mental_models.name: Technology Adoption Curve
mental_models.description: Viewing the acceptance of new medical interventions (like vaccines) through the lens of consumer technology adoption, categorizing the population into first adopters, the willing majority, the hesitant, and the resistant.
mental_models.example: Explaining that just like with a new cell phone, first adopters jump in immediately, the majority follows after seeing it work, while a small percentage remains permanently hesitant or resistant.

mental_models.name: Mental Health as an Underground River
mental_models.description: A metaphor for understanding mental health not as a constant, visible state, but as an underlying vulnerability that flows beneath the surface of a person's life and emerges during periods of high stress.
mental_models.example: Moffitt notes that mental health is an 'underground river that runs through the life course and at times of intense stress it bubbles up to the surface and that happens for all of us.'

heuristics: If you haven't had your turn at a mental disorder yet, it's probably coming to you.
Under stress is when executive functions' rubber hits the road.

signature_quotes.text: if you haven't had your turn at mental disorder yet it's probably coming to you
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the finding that 85% of people experience a mental health disorder by age 50.

signature_quotes.text: mental health is a kind of a a a river that runs underneath the uh the you know underground river that runs through the life course and at times of intense stress it bubbles up to the surface
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how mental health challenges manifest throughout a person's life.

signature_quotes.text: people simply do not remember their childhood and Adolescent adolescence the way it was
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the unreliability of retrospective recall in psychological studies.

signature_quotes.text: when under stress that's when executive functions rubber hits the road and I don't think we do a good job of testing that in the laboratory
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the gap between clinical tests of executive function and real-world application.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on point-in-time surveys to understand complex behavioral resistance (like vaccine refusal).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: People who are resistant to the behavior are often also resistant to taking surveys, leading to skewed data. Furthermore, people invent 'here and now' rationalizations for decisions that are actually rooted in lifelong psychological traits.

anti_patterns.description: Using retrospective recall to measure past mental health or cognitive abilities.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Humans naturally recast their memories to view their past more favorably, forgetting past mental disorders and inflating their historical abilities (like reading level or height).

anti_patterns.description: Designing public health messaging without considering the cognitive processing and personality styles of the target audience.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Standard messaging relies on facts and logic, which overwhelms people with slower processing speeds or deep-seated mistrust. Meanwhile, anti-messaging succeeds because it perfectly targets their feelings of fear, anger, and alienation.
